actions.thought: Jack-Ten suited is a strong, playable hand in late position. As the dealer/button, I have the advantage of acting last post-flop. Opening with a standard raise of 2.5x (250 chips) is a solid aggressive strategy to either win the blinds immediately or build a pot to play with initiative against the opponents.

8c 3s is an extremely weak hand, especially when facing a raise from the button. There is no reason to defend this hand out of position; folding is the standard and most profitable play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

3d 7d is a very weak suited hand. Even though I am in the Big Blind facing a Button open, 73s has poor playability out of position and low equity against a typical Button raising range. It's best to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

7-3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. Even from the button, it is standard to fold this hand rather than open-raising or calling, as it rarely wins at showdown and is difficult to play post-flop.

We are in the Small Blind with 8c 7d, which is an unsuited and weak hand. The Button has folded, so it's a battle of the blinds. While we could try to steal or limp, 87o is simply too weak to play profitably out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the safest and most optimal play to preserve our stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-four suited in a 3-handed game. This is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even though I have position, opening with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. Folding is the best decision.

I am in the small blind with a strong hand, Ace-Queen offsuit, and the button has folded. This gives me a high probability of having the best hand heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal strategy to capitalize on this strength, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds uncontested or set up a strong post-flop scenario. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure.

I am holding 2c 7d, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Small Blind has raised to 300. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400 (getting 2:1 odds), this hand has very poor playability and equity. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Td-9c. In a 3-handed game, this hand has decent playability and is well within my opening range from the button. I want to be aggressive early in the tournament to put pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop. Raising to 250 is a standard open size to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 4d, which is a very weak hand. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, and since I am out of position and my hand has almost no equity or playability, folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5d, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the Button, this hand falls well outside of any reasonable defending range. The easiest and most correct play is to fold.

Ten-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game from the button. Given the previous hands where Flashlight has shown significant aggression by raising frequently and my need to conserve chips for more favorable spots, there is no justification for entering the pot with this range of cards. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

With Flash folding on the button, the action is on me in the small blind. I hold 6d3d, which is a weak, disconnected suited hand. Playing this hand out of position against the big blind is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the optimal and standard decision here.

Ten-Seven suited is a standard open from the Button in a 3-handed game. It plays decently post-flop, can make strong flushes and straights, and provides good bluffing and stealing equity pre-flop. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

Q6o is a weak hand to play, especially when out of position in the small blind against an opening raise from the button. Calling would require playing out of position against a range that likely dominates my queen, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary given my stack size and current position. The most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 8s, which is an extremely weak hand. Pro raised to 250 from the Button. Although it only costs 150 more to call, playing this hand out of position against a raise is unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Opening the pot with a raise allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop hand with the advantage of position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce suited (Kd 2d). The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 2.5bb. While King-Deuce suited has some value in a 3-handed game, it is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opener, especially with a professional player still to act in the Big Blind. To avoid a difficult post-flop situation and protect my stack early in the tournament, I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better position.
Facing a 2.5bb button open from an active player, K7o is too weak and unplayable post-flop to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit (J3o). While the button is the best position to steal the blinds, J3o is a very weak hand and does not perform well post-flop. Given that Flashlight (in the Big Blind) has shown significant aggression in previous hands, a steal attempt is likely to be met with resistance. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 7s, which is a very weak and unconnected offsuit hand. The Button has folded, so it's just me and the Big Blind. Given the poor equity of 74o, the best play is to fold and save my chips for a better starting hand.

AhTh is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Since I am on the Button and first to act, an open-raise is mandatory. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to 250 chips to steal the blinds or build a pot in position with a strong suited Ace.

I am in the small blind with King of Diamonds and Ten of Spades, a solid hand in a three-handed game. The button (Pro) has been active and aggressive, making a raise to 250. While KTs is a strong hand, 3-betting from out of position can lead to difficult post-flop situations if called. Calling allows me to see a flop with a good range while maintaining a reasonable pot-to-stack ratio. I will call the 200 to complete the 250 bet.

I have Ace-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button raised and the Small Blind called. I am getting 5-to-1 pot odds (150 to win a total pot of 750). This is a very favorable price to see a flop with an Ace. While my kicker is weak, folding would be too tight in this spot, and raising is unnecessary given I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I will call to close the action.

The flop is 6s 9d Qc, which completely misses my hand of Kd Ts. I have no pair and no strong draws. As I am out of position against two other players, including the original pre-flop aggressor who likely has a range that connects well with this board, betting would be an ill-advised bluff. The optimal strategy here is to check and re-evaluate based on the subsequent action, likely folding if there is a significant bet.

On this dry flop of Q-9-6 rainbow, my Ace-high has very little value and almost no drawing potential. Having called from the Big Blind pre-flop, I'm facing two opponents. Flashlight (SB) has already checked. Betting into two players here with just Ace-high and a weak kicker would be a mistake. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression from the original raiser (Pro) or the SB, as this board hits their ranges better than mine.

With AhTh on a 6s 9d Qc board, I completely missed and have no meaningful draws in a 3-way pot. The flop texture connects well with the blinds' pre-flop calling ranges. C-betting here into two opponents with just Ace-high and no immediate equity is a low-EV play. Taking a free card by checking behind is the best option to try and hit an Ace or Ten on the turn.

The board has paired on the turn with the Queen of Hearts, and I hold King-Ten offsuit with no pair and no real draw. Checking is the only logical play here as I am first to act and have zero showdown value. I do not want to put more chips into the pot with a weak hand, so I will check and look to fold if either opponent decides to bet.

The turn brings a second Queen, pairing the board. After everyone checked the flop, the Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked again. My Ace-high holds some showdown value in a pot where no one has yet shown aggression post-flop. I will check to see what the original pre-flop raiser (Pro) does. Betting here doesn't achieve much as better hands like middle pairs are unlikely to fold, and I'd prefer to see a free river or evaluate a small bet from Pro rather than bloating the pot out of position.

Both opponents have checked twice, indicating weakness or occasionally a trap with a Queen. The Queen pairing on the turn makes it less likely either of them has a Q. However, my Ace-high still holds decent showdown value, and bluffing into two opponents on a paired board might only get called by better hands like pocket pairs, 9x, or 6x. Checking again exercises pot control, allows me to realize my equity for free, and gives me a chance to hit an Ace or Ten on the river.

The board is 6s 9d Qc Qh 3h and I hold Kd Ts. The action has checked through on both the flop and the turn, which indicates that nobody has a strong made hand or everyone is pot-controlling. My King-high is unlikely to win at showdown if anyone decides to value bet, and bluffing with King-high into two opponents on the river is futile. The most standard and conservative play is to check, aiming to see a cheap showdown or fold if facing a bet.

The board is 6-9-Q-Q-3 and the action has been checked all the way from the flop to the river. I have Ace-high (Ac 4d). In a three-way pot where nobody has shown any aggression post-flop, Ace-high has a reasonable amount of showdown value. Betting now is unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a 6 or a 9) and will only be called by hands that beat me. Flashlight (SB) has checked for the third time. I will check and hope to see a free showdown. If Pro (the original raiser) bets behind me, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

With Ace-high and both opponents checking down all three streets, my hand possesses solid showdown value. Attempting to bluff now is risky, as it might only get called by hands that beat me, such as a small pair, a 6, or a 9, while folding out hands I already beat, like King-high or missed draws. Checking behind ensures we reach showdown without risking more chips, which is the most optimal play.

Qh-Jc is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, particularly on the button. Opening here is the standard play to apply pressure to the blinds and maintain position. I will stick with my standard open size of 250 chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 5s, a weak offsuit connector. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 250. Calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding that doesn't flop well. Given the stack sizes and the presence of a competent player (Pro) in the Big Blind, folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

K9o is a marginal offsuit hand. Even though Flashlight is opening frequently from the Button, calling out of position with an easily dominated offsuit broadway hand leads to negative expected value. Reverse implied odds are high, and post-flop playability is poor. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here, preserving our stack for better opportunities where we have suited cards, better high-card strength, or positional advantage.

On the button in a 3-handed game, J3 suited is a marginal hand that borders between an open-raise and a fold. Given the 100BB stack depths and the fact that I want to maintain a solid image, I will choose to fold this hand. Although suited, the Trey kicker is extremely weak, which often leads to being out-kicked or dominated if I pair the Jack. I'll wait for a more robust hand to open-raise from this position.

I am in the Small Blind with Q4o, which is a weak offsuit hand. The Button has folded, but playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with 7h 9c. 79o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand and does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening even in short-handed play. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am in the small blind with suited connectors (Tc, 8c) heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. Completing the blind is a standard, low-risk way to see a flop with a hand that has decent post-flop playability. Calling to complete the bet is the most prudent strategy here to realize equity without inflating the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with J6o. Flashlight (Small Blind) has just called the completion of the blind after the Button folded. J6o is a weak hand to raise with in this spot. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, checking is the most logical and standard play to realize my equity for free.

The flop is paired with nines and has a seven. I have an open-ended straight draw, needing a six or a jack to complete the sequence. As I am the first to act from the small blind, checking is the optimal strategy. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially take a free card if my opponent also checks behind. Betting here would likely just commit more chips into a board where the opponent could easily have a nine or a pocket pair, making a check the most rational play to realize my equity.

On a paired flop of 9-9-7, I have Jack-high with no draw. The Small Blind checked to me in a limped pot. While I could attempt a bluff, checking back is the most prudent play to see a turn card for free. There is no reason to inflate the pot with a weak hand when I have some marginal showdown value against total air.

I have completed a straight (6-7-8-9-10) on the turn. Although the board is paired with nines and has a flush draw available, my straight is very strong. I am currently first to act and should bet for value to get paid by players with lower pairs, trips, or straight/flush draws. A bet of 150 into a 200 pot is a solid sizing to extract value while maintaining control.

On the turn 6d, I have improved to bottom pair (sixes) with a Jack kicker. The board is quite coordinated with a pair of nines, a potential flush, and several straight possibilities (85, 8T). Flashlight's lead for 150 into 200 represents strength, but in a limped pot, ranges are extremely wide. I have enough showdown value to call one bet to see a river, as folding a pair immediately to a single bet might be too exploitable, though I must be cautious of further aggression on the river.

The board shows 9d-9s-7d-6d-Jh, and I hold Tc-8c for a ten-high straight. While the board presents possibilities for a flush or a full house, my straight remains a very strong holding. Given that I led the turn action and was called, I should continue to represent strength and extract value on the river. A half-pot bet (250 into 500) is a balanced sizing that encourages calls from hands like trips, lower straights, or two-pair combinations that might feel priced in to look me up.

I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Nines) on the river. While the board is coordinated with straight and flush possibilities and my kicker is the 7 from the board, the pot odds (3:1) make a fold too tight in a limped pot where ranges are extremely wide. Flashlight's lead on the turn and river could represent a wide variety of hands, including bluffs, smaller pairs, or missed draws. While I lose to any 9, a flush, a straight, or a Jack with a better kicker (8 or higher), the strength of my hand relative to a limped range justifies a call.

Pocket fours are a strong enough hand to open from the button, especially with 100BB stacks. Raising allows me to take initiative and potentially take down the blinds or play in position with a pair, which has decent equity against most range-appropriate hands in this spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 86o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 2.5x. While Flashlight has been active, 86o is too weak to defend from the Small Blind out of position against a button open, as it lacks both high card value and connectivity/suitability. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips.
9c 2s is a very weak starting hand. Facing a raise to 250 from the Button, the most mathematically sound and strategic decision is to fold and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong premium hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 88 big blinds. Standard play here is to raise to build the pot and take the initiative. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to exert pressure and extract value from worse hands that might call from the blinds.

7h4h is a weak suited one-gapper that plays very poorly out of position, especially facing a 3bb open from the Button. Defending this hand in the Small Blind would lead to difficult post-flop decisions against a potentially stronger range. The standard and most profitable play is to fold and wait for better starting hands.

Defending the big blind against a button open is a standard play to prevent giving up the pot too easily, especially with the given pot odds. While 69 offsuit is a marginal hand, the cost to call is 200 chips to win a total pot of 650, which provides sufficient justification to see a flop and evaluate the board texture.

I hold top pair with a weak 6-kicker on a relatively dry board of 7-3-9. As the out-of-position player who defended the big blind against a button raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to gauge their strength based on their continuation bet size without bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Queen offsuit. The flop of 7-3-9 rainbow is relatively dry and unlikely to have hit the big blind's calling range hard. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will put in a standard continuation bet of about half the pot to take the initiative and potentially win the hand immediately, or extract value from weaker Ace-high hands or backdoor draws.

I hold top pair (nines) on a dry, rainbow board of 7-3-9. The opponent's continuation bet of 300 into a 650 pot is relatively small, which suggests they could be betting with overcards, a weak pair, or a made hand. Folding here would be too exploitable given my top pair strength. Raising is not optimal as it risks bloating the pot with a medium-strength hand and might isolate me against better holdings. Calling is the most disciplined action to keep the pot manageable and see a turn card, where I can re-evaluate based on the opponent's next move.

The turn card is a Queen, which significantly strengthens the opponent's range, as many of their pre-flop button opening hands (like AQ, KQ, or QJ) now have a pair better than my nines. As the out-of-position player, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard strategic play. This allows me to gauge their action on the turn while keeping the pot size manageable. If they check behind, I maintain control; if they bet, I can evaluate the sizing relative to my hand's strength.

I have improved to Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) with the Queen of Spades on the turn. The board is now 7-3-9-Q. My opponent, Flashlight, has checked to me twice. Given the pre-flop action and the flop call, Flashlight likely has a marginal hand like a pair of nines or sevens, or perhaps a straight draw. I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of approximately 60% of the pot (around 750) is a strong value bet that can be called by worse pairs while making it expensive for draws to continue.

The turn card (Queen) significantly improves the opponent's button-raising range, making my pair of nines extremely vulnerable. The opponent's bet of 750 into a 1200 pot demonstrates strength. Given the board texture and the likelihood that the opponent has a pair of queens or better, or even an overpair, calling here is not mathematically sound. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable spots.

Playing 3-handed on the button, T6s is a weak hand (four-gapper) with poor playability. It is often dominated and does not warrant an open-raise, even with the positional advantage. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to attack the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand (8-3 offsuit). There is no incentive to invest more chips into this pot given the poor cards and the position. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit, which is a weak, uncoordinated hand that plays poorly in most scenarios. To maximize my stack over the long run, I must maintain discipline and avoid playing marginal or trash hands from early position (or here, the button opener). Folding is the only profitable long-term strategy with this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt to steal the blind from 'Pro' in the Big Blind, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop if called. Given that we are early in the tournament and I want to maintain my stack, I will choose to fold rather than limp or raise with such a marginal hand.

I am on the button with 8c4s, which is a very weak starting hand even in short-handed play. While opening the button is generally profitable, this specific hand lacks both high card value and connectivity. Given the stack depths and the fact that Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to play back or call, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

With King-Ten offsuit in the small blind and the button folded, I have a strong enough hand to attack the big blind. Raising puts pressure on the big blind to defend with weaker holdings and gives me the initiative. I will raise to 3 big blinds (300) to steal or play a solid hand post-flop if called.

Defending the big blind with Jack-Seven suited against a small blind raise is a standard strategic move. The pot odds are 2:1, providing sufficient justification to call and see a flop. Raising would likely be overplaying the hand given the strength distribution of potential SB opening ranges, and folding would be too passive.

I flopped the nut straight (K-Q-J-T-9) on a rainbow board. This is a phenomenal situation, and my main goal is to build the pot to extract maximum value. The 9-J-Q board connects heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, meaning he likely has a piece of this flop, such as top pair (like AQ/KQ), two pair (like QJ/J9), or strong straight draws. Betting is essential here to get value from these hands and start building a large pot. A half-pot bet of 300 is a solid sizing that gets value while keeping his calling range wide.

I have a pair of Jacks with a Seven kicker on a board of 9-J-Q. The board is somewhat connected, but my middle pair is likely ahead of many of the Small Blind's bluffs or weaker holdings in this spot. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or cause me to overplay my strength, and folding a pair of Jacks here would be overly conservative given the pot odds. Calling allows me to evaluate the turn while keeping the pot manageable.

I hold the nut straight, but the turn pairs the 9, making a full house possible for my opponent (e.g., J9, Q9). However, full houses make up a very small portion of his range. He is much more likely to hold trips (like A9 or T9), two pair (like QJ), top pair, or a newly formed heart flush draw. It is crucial to continue betting for value and to charge any draws. A half-pot bet of 600 is sized perfectly to extract value from these weaker hands while cautiously navigating the paired board.

The board shows 9d-Jc-Qh-9h, and I have Jh-7h, giving me a pair of Jacks. The opponent has bet 600 into a 1200 pot, offering me 3:1 odds. While the turn card (9h) pairs the board, making it possible for the opponent to have trips, my pair of Jacks remains a viable hand. Folding here is too conservative, and raising would likely only be called by better hands. Calling is the most strategic choice to keep the pot manageable and see the river.

The river 7d is a relatively safe card, bringing no flush possibilities. While the board is paired and full houses are a possibility, my King-high straight remains an exceptionally strong hand. The opponent's calling range on the flop and turn includes many hands that will happily call a river bet, such as trips (A9, K9, T9), strong two pairs (QJ), or lower straights (T8). A half-pot value bet of 1200 targets these second-best hands to extract maximum value, while also ensuring I don't bloat the pot unnecessarily in case my opponent managed to river a full house or already had one.

I hold two pair (Jacks and Nines, utilizing the pair of nines on the board). The river card (7d) does not improve my hand, but it also doesn't complete the obvious flush draws. While any Queen or Trip Nines beats me, the opponent's betting pattern (1200 into a 3600 pot) provides 3:1 pot odds. Given the frequency of semi-bluffs in this format and the fact that I have a reasonably strong hand, folding here would be too exploitable. I will make the call to see the showdown.

Q5 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. While we are on the Button and could attempt a steal, this hand plays quite poorly post-flop and will often be dominated if called or 3-bet. A standard fold is the best play here.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Raising instead of limping allows me to take the initiative and build a pot with a superior holding. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing to pressure the big blind and define the ranges.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Flashlight has raised from the Small Blind to 300. Even with pot odds of 2:1 and the game being 3-handed, 62o has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff raise here.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game provides a significant positional advantage, allowing me to open a wide range of hands. While Jh-5h is not a premium hand, it is suited and holds enough equity to warrant an open-raise to steal the blinds, as I have been playing aggressively in this position throughout the session.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 8s, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. Playing this hand out of position against a Button raise is generally unprofitable, especially with a strong player like Pro still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold to conserve my stack.

Pocket eights is a solid middle pair. Against a button raise, it is well ahead of the opponent's opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to deny equity and build the pot, flat-calling from the big blind keeps the opponent's weaker hands in the pot, controls the size of the pot out of position, and allows us to see a flop to evaluate whether to proceed, primarily looking for a set or an undercard board.

On this Ace-high flop, our middle pair of eights is vulnerable, and the standard play out of position as the pre-flop caller is to check to the aggressor. Checking allows us to pot control and see how Flashlight responds. Leading out (donking) wouldn't accomplish much, as worse hands will fold and better hands (like any Ace, or higher pocket pairs) will call or raise.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-high board. Since my opponent called my pre-flop raise from the big blind, they likely have a significant number of Aces in their range, which makes my pair of fives extremely vulnerable. Betting here would likely be a bluff that gets called by any Ace or better. Checking behind is the most prudent play to control the pot size and evaluate future streets while hoping to improve my hand or get to a cheap showdown.

With the turn bringing the 3h, our pocket eights are still a marginal made hand. The opponent checked back the flop, suggesting he either has a weak Ace, a mid-tier pocket pair, or missed broadway cards. While we could bet to deny equity to overcards and draws, checking again allows us to control the pot size out of position and potentially induce bluffs from his air. If we bet, we mostly get called by better hands and fold out worse ones.

I have a pair of fives and a flush draw on a board of 5d, 6s, Ah, 3h. Since the turn was checked through, I have the option to take a free card to potentially hit my flush or improve my pair, keeping the pot size manageable. Checking behind is the most disciplined move here as it allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot against a potentially stronger Ace, while also controlling the game state heading to the river.

The board texture of A-6-5-3-4 is extremely coordinated, presenting a four-card straight where any 2 or 7 makes a straight. My pocket eights have some showdown value against missed broadway hands (like KQ/QJ) that checked through the flop and turn. Betting here has very little value since worse hands will almost certainly fold, and better hands (like an Ace, a higher pocket pair, or a straight) will call or raise. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate if facing a river bet.

The board shows 3-4-5-6-A, meaning any 2 or 7 completes a straight. I have a pair of fives, which loses to any Ace, two-pair, or straight. My opponent checked, which is common with weak hands, but could also be a trap with an Ace or a straight. Since I cannot reasonably get called by a hand worse than mine, betting provides no value. Checking behind is the most disciplined way to reach showdown.

In a 3-handed game, K7o on the button is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I have position on both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Flashlight). A standard 2.5x raise allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if called.
Holding Ten-Three offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. This hand is extremely weak and offers no playability, making it a very straightforward fold.

Holding Eight of Clubs and Six of Diamonds in the big blind, I have a very weak, offsuit hand with poor playability. While the pot odds to call are decent (150 to win a total pot of 550), playing weak hands out of position against a button raise is generally negative expected value. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

On the button in a 3-handed game, A6o is definitely strong enough to open. Raising to 2.5x (250 chips) to put pressure on the blinds and potentially steal the pot pre-flop. If called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

K3o is a very weak hand, especially when out of position against an opening raise from the button. Calling here would put me in a disadvantaged position for the rest of the hand without a strong holding. Given my stack size and the strength of the opponent's opening range, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision to preserve my chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x. While the pot odds are roughly 2.7 to 1, T5o is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against a player with a larger stack. I have not been involved in many pots recently, but this is not the right hand to start defending. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K-3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in a three-handed game from the button. There is no strategic value in opening this hand, and folding is the most disciplined and correct choice to preserve my stack.

I have 9-5 offsuit in the small blind. This is a very weak hand with poor post-flop potential. Given that I would be out of position against the big blind (Pro), who has a substantial stack and has shown aggression in previous hands, it is best to fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding Ace-high on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot, benefiting from being in position if I am called.

I am in the Small Blind with an offsuit weak connector (4c 5s) facing a pre-flop raise from the Button. This hand has very poor equity and playability out of position against a standard opening range. The mathematically correct and standard play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind and facing a 2.5x open from the Button. I have K8o, which is a marginal hand but holds enough equity to defend against a standard button open given the pot odds (calling 150 into a 400 pot). Folding is too tight, and raising is not optimal as this hand does not play well in 3-bet pots out of position. Calling is the most balanced and profitable approach.

The flop is extremely favorable to the button's opening range, as it contains an Ace and a Queen. My hand, King-high with a single backdoor flush draw, has virtually no showdown value and minimal equity against a continuation bet. Betting would be reckless as it provides no value and little fold equity. Checking is the standard play here to see the turn cheaply or fold if facing significant pressure.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of Ac-Qc-7s. Although my kicker (3) is weak, I am the pre-flop aggressor and have the positional advantage. Flashlight checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 55% of the pot to extract value from draws (like clubs or straight draws) and weaker pairs (like a Queen), while also protecting my hand and maintaining the initiative.

The flop texture containing an Ace and a Queen is extremely favorable for the button's opening range. With only King-high and a marginal backdoor flush draw, I lack the necessary equity to profitably call a 300-chip bet into a 550-chip pot. Given the tournament context and the strength of the board relative to the button's likely holdings, folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient play.

I have King-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to take control of the hand, try to steal the blinds, or play a decent pot in position against the blinds if called. A raise to 250 is a standard 2.5x open.

I am in the small blind with 4d-Td. While these cards are suited, they are relatively weak and I am playing out of position against the button. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and there is no reason to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

In a 3-handed game, Q8o is a decent hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open. I am getting favorable pot odds (150 to call into a pot that will be 550) and while I will be out of position, folding is too tight against a standard button opening range. I will call and see a flop.

I am first to act on a flop of Ac-4h-9h. I hold Qc-8h, which has completely missed this board. The Ace-high texture significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro on the Button). There is no reason to lead out (donk bet) into the aggressor with zero equity. I will check and plan to fold if faced with a bet, as I have no viable draws or pair potential to continue.

On this A-4-9 flop with a flush draw, I completely missed and have no meaningful equity besides a backdoor straight draw. While an Ace heavily favors my pre-flop raising range and a c-bet could fold out some better high-card hands or underpairs, checking behind is safer. It allows me to pot control, see a free turn card, and avoid getting check-raised when I have virtually zero equity.

The turn brings the King of hearts, completing a potential flush and adding another overcard to the board. I have no made hand and a very weak flush draw (8-high). Given that the pre-flop aggressor checked back on the flop, they might have a marginal hand or missed entirely, but betting here into a board that favors the button's range is risky. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

The turn brings the King of Hearts, giving me second pair but also completing a possible flush draw as there are now three hearts on the board along with an Ace. Flash has checked twice, indicating weakness or a trap. Since I have showdown value but little chance of getting called by worse hands if I bet, and a high risk of getting check-raised by flushes or Aces, checking behind is the best line for pot control.

The board is Ac-4h-9h-Kh-5c and I hold Qc-8h. I defended the Big Blind pre-flop and both the Button (Pro) and I have checked the flop and turn. The Button's consecutive checks suggest a capped range, likely consisting of middle pairs, weak showdown value, or total air. While I have no made hand, my Queen-high has a small chance of winning at showdown against some of Pro's missed straight draws or lower high-card hands. Betting here as a bluff is an option, but since the pot is small and I have some very marginal showdown value, checking is the most prudent play. I will check and hope to get to a free showdown or fold if Pro decides to bet.

With second pair on an Ace-high board with three hearts, I have decent showdown value but not enough to value bet, as worse hands will mostly fold and better hands (like an Ace or a flush) will call or raise. Checking behind ensures I get to showdown safely and avoids getting check-raised by a disguised strong hand.

I am dealt Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand that performs exceptionally well in position. Given my position on the button, raising is the standard and optimal play to take the betting lead and build a pot with my strong holding. I will use my standard open raise sizing of 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has opened for 250 (2.5x the big blind). J7o is too weak to defend against a button raise, especially while out of position and with a strong player in the Big Blind still to act. It's a clear fold to preserve chips for better spots.

T8o is a weak offsuit holding that plays poorly out of position. Despite getting decent odds against a button open, defending this hand usually leads to negative EV situations post-flop. Folding and preserving the stack for better spots is the optimal play.
I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and put pressure on the blinds while maintaining positional advantage.

I am holding 2s 3c in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak starting hand. The Button (Flash) has raised to 3 big blinds. With terrible hole cards and bad position, the only correct play is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 8-6 offsuit, a very weak, uncoordinated hand. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), 8-6 offsuit has very little equity post-flop, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Defending the big blind requires better hand selection. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

J8 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, even when playing from the button. Raising here is an unnecessary risk, and calling with an offsuit gapper isn't profitable long-term. The standard play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the small blind with 6-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability. Even though I have already posted 50 chips, investing more money to complete the blind is not a profitable move given the strength of the hand. Folding is the standard and correct strategic play here.

I am dealt Queen-three offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand with poor playability and low showdown value. In a three-handed game, there is no need to enter the pot with such marginal holdings, especially when I can wait for better cards to capitalize on my position. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has folded on the button, leaving only me and Pro (the chip leader) in the Big Blind. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 92o plays very poorly out of position and is unlikely to win a significant pot or successfully bluff against a competent opponent. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9d 2d. While being suited provides some playability, 9-2 is one of the weakest starting hands. In a 3-handed game, I should open a wide range from the button, but 92s is still near the bottom of my range and doesn't justify a steal attempt here against opponents who have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand to play out of position from the small blind. The most prudent action is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than risking chips on a marginal steal attempt.

With A8 offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, we have a clear opening hand. A standard 2.5x raise puts pressure on the blinds while keeping the sizing reasonable. We have the chip lead and maintaining pre-flop aggression in late position is crucial for accumulating chips.

The button (Pro) opened with a raise, which indicates a wide opening range. With King-Ten offsuit in the small blind, I have a decent hand that plays well enough to see a flop. Calling is efficient here to maintain a reasonable pot-to-stack ratio and see how the hand develops, as KTo is not strong enough to warrant a 3-bet bluff against an active opponent out of position. The price is reasonable, offering decent pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to put in 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 750. While the pot odds are roughly 4:1 (20% equity needed), K2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. It is easily dominated by better Kings (K9+, KQ, AK) and has very little playability on most boards. Given the reverse implied odds of hitting a King with a deuce kicker, I choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold top pair (tens) and a king-high flush draw on a board with three hearts. As the out-of-position player, checking is the optimal standard move. It allows me to evaluate the pre-flop aggressor's (the button) intention, control the pot size, and potentially realize my strong equity with the flush draw if the opponent checks back or continuation bets.

We completely missed this monotone heart flop with As8c, and crucially, we don't hold a heart. Betting here as a continuation bet would be a low-percentage bluff, as opponents often continue on monotone boards if they have a single heart, a pair, or a straight draw. Checking behind is the safest option to see a free turn card, control the pot, and avoid spewing chips when we have very low equity.

The turn card, the Jack of Clubs, gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) in addition to my nut flush draw (holding the King of Hearts) and my pair of Tens. The opponent checked behind on the flop, which often signifies a capped or weak range, or perhaps a draw. With significant equity, I should take the betting lead to build the pot or pressure the opponent to fold. A bet of half the pot is a standard, effective size to balance value and protection while maintaining control.

With As8c on a 3h-9h-Th-Jc board, we have an open-ended straight draw, but we hold no hearts. This board is extremely dangerous as a flush is already possible, and any heart that completes our straight could give our opponent a flush. Additionally, we could be up against higher straight draws or made straights like KQ or Q8. Facing a half-pot lead from Flashlight on this wet and highly coordinated board, continuing with just Ace-high and heavily discounted outs with severe reverse implied odds would be a mistake. Folding is the safest and most optimal play.

7c-Td is a weak, unsuited hand that lacks connectivity and high-card strength. Despite being on the button, playing this hand from the start is negative expected value. Preserving my stack for stronger holdings is the correct strategic approach.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o after the Button has folded. K9o is a solid hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot immediately or take the initiative into the flop.

JcQc is a premium suited connector that plays excellently in position against a Small Blind open. Flash's range is likely wide here as a steal attempt. By calling, I keep their weaker hands in the pot and can utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

The flop is As-6h-6c, which is a very dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have an advantage on Ace-high boards. However, with King-high and no real draw, I will check to see how the Big Blind reacts. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free turn card if the opponent also checks. If the opponent bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but I'm likely to fold as I have very little equity against a range that continues.

Flash checked the flop on an A-6-6 board. They could be pot-controlling a marginal hand like a pocket pair, trapping with an Ace, or giving up on a missed steal. With Queen-Jack high and backdoor draws, I have some potential but not enough equity to confidently value bet or bluff right now. Checking behind keeps the pot small, allows me to see a free turn card to potentially improve, and gather more information on Flash's range.

The board has double-paired with Aces and Sixes (As-6h-6c-Ad). I currently hold King-high. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, checking the flop and now checking the turn is the most sensible approach. My hand has very little showdown value against any pocket pair or any piece of the board, but betting here is unlikely to represent enough strength to fold out a better hand given how dry the board is. I will check to see the river for free or evaluate if the Big Blind decides to bet.

Flash's second check on this A-6-6-A board strongly indicates they do not have an Ace, as they would likely bet for value by now. Because the board is double-paired with Aces and Sixes, any pocket pair from Deuces to Jacks is counterfeited and forced to play the board with their highest card as a kicker (e.g., 77 plays as A-A-6-6-7). My Queen-Jack plays as A-A-6-6-Q, meaning I actually have showdown value against lower pocket pairs and lower missed broadway cards. While I could bet to try and fold out King-high, checking keeps the pot small, avoids the risk of being check-raised by a slow-played Ace, and lets me see the river for free.

The board has run out As-6h-6c-Ad-Kd, meaning the best possible hand is four-of-a-kind Aces or a full house. I hold Kh-9d, which gives me two pair (Aces and Kings) with a 9 kicker. Since the King is on the board, anyone with a card higher than a 9 (T, J, Q) will beat my 9 kicker if they also play the board's AA-KK. Given that Pro checked both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely they hold an Ace or a 6, but they could easily have a better kicker or a small pocket pair that now plays as two pair with a better kicker. Betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand and will only be called by hands that beat me or split. I will check and hope to win at showdown or split the pot.

On this A-6-6-A-K board, my Queen-Jack is completely counterfeited, meaning my best five-card hand is simply playing the board (Two Pair, Aces and Sixes, with a King kicker). If I check, I will chop the pot against all of Flash's missed broadway hands and lower pocket pairs (22-55) that also play the board. While a bet could potentially bluff out medium pocket pairs (77-QQ) that currently beat the board, it risks getting called by a King, an Ace, or a stubborn pocket pair. Since I have significant chop equity and the pot is small, checking behind to take the free showdown is the safest and most optimal play.

I am on the button with pocket eights (8s 8d), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to build a pot and capitalize on my position. A standard open raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) is appropriate here to narrow the field or win the blinds immediately.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind. Although 6h7h is a nice suited connector, calling leaves us out of position post-flop and invites the Big Blind to squeeze. 3-betting is an option, but considering our conservative strategy and desire to protect our stack from OOP bloat, folding is the disciplined choice.

Pocket nines are a strong starting hand in a heads-up scenario against the button. Given the opponent's high frequency of opening raises, I have a strong equity advantage. 3-betting allows me to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially take it down pre-flop or define the opponent's range more effectively.

I started the action with a standard raise to 300 from the button with pocket eights. Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. In a three-handed game, pocket eights is a very strong hand, but 4-betting risks a shove from a narrow range of better pairs or overcards. Calling allows me to play the pot in position and evaluate the flop. I have a healthy stack and plenty of room to play post-flop.

The flop is Q-J-4 with two diamonds, which is a wet board that connects well with the opponent's calling range after I 3-bet pre-flop. My pocket nines are currently a middle pair, vulnerable to any overcards (A, K, T) or higher pairs that the opponent might have in their range (such as Qx or Jx). Betting into this board on the flop is unlikely to get value from worse hands and will likely force me to fold to aggression from better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see if the opponent checks back or evaluates the turn, effectively playing as a bluff catcher for now.

On a board of 4d-Qd-Js, my pocket eights are now an underpair to two overcards. Flashlight, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked to me. This check could indicate a variety of hands, including missed high cards (like AK or AT), a medium-strength hand (like a weak Jack or a flush draw), or a potential trap. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and avoid being check-raised off the hand. Given the coordinated board and the pre-flop action, pocket eights have some showdown value but are not strong enough to bet for value against most of the range that calls. I will take the free card and evaluate the turn.

The board is Q-J-4-8. My pocket nines have limited showdown value but are highly vulnerable to overcards and any Queen or Jack in the opponent's range. Since both players checked the flop, the pot remains manageable. Betting now is unlikely to elicit calls from worse hands, while checking allows me to control the pot size and act as a bluff catcher.

I have turned a set of eights, which is a massive strength increase from my previous position. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop but has now checked both the flop and the turn. This suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like AK, TT, or a weak Queen/Jack) or is giving up on the hand. I need to bet now to extract value from those marginal hands and to charge potential draws (diamonds or straight draws like KT/T9). Checking back again would risk giving a free card on a very coordinated board. I will bet 1,000 into the 1,850 pot to build the pot and protect my equity.

The opponent, on the button, checked the flop and decided to bet the turn on a Q-J-4-8 board. My pocket nines are effectively a bluff catcher here, but against a button opener who may be betting a wide range of semi-bluffs (such as flush draws or straight draws) or weaker made hands that they want to test, calling is mathematically justified given the pot odds. With a pot of 1850, I need to call 1000, which requires roughly 26% equity to break even. Given my 3-bet pre-flop, the opponent's range is somewhat narrowed, but there is still plenty of room for hands I currently beat or that are drawing. Folding is too tight given the current pot odds, and raising would likely only isolate me against stronger hands. I will call to see a river card and re-evaluate.

The river card is the 3h, which does not improve my hand. My pocket nines are essentially a weak bluff-catcher on a board of Q-J-4-8-3. Since I am out of position and the opponent was the aggressor on the turn, I have no reason to lead out with a bet, as I would only be called by better hands. I will check and evaluate the opponent's action, intending to fold to any significant bet given the board texture.

I have a set of eights on a board where the most obvious draws (flush and open-ended straight) have missed. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop, checked the flop, and then called my 1,000 bet on the turn. His check on the river suggests he is in 'check-call' mode with a hand like top pair (AQ, KQ), an overpair (AA, KK), or two-pair (QJ). I have a very strong hand that beats almost everything in his range except for pocket Queens or Jacks. I will bet 2,200 for value, aiming to get called by his top pair or overpair hands while maximizing the pot size.

The board of Q-J-4-8-3 is dangerous for pocket nines, as any queen, jack, or even an eight or lower pair beats me. While I have pot odds to call, the opponent's turn and river aggression suggest they have a hand stronger than my bluff-catcher. Given the board texture and the aggressive line taken by the opponent, I do not believe I have the required equity to make a profitable call. Folding is the most prudent decision to protect my remaining stack for future hands.

King-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand even on the button in a 3-handed game. While we have position, opening with weak offsuit kings often leads to marginal post-flop situations when called, and we don't block much besides a king. Folding is the safest and most standard play here to conserve chips.

With Ace-King offsuit, I have a very strong hand for a three-handed game. Since the button has folded, I want to take the initiative and put pressure on the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that allows me to build the pot with my premium hand and either take it down pre-flop or play a strong heads-up pot.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Big Blind with Q9o. Flashlight (Small Blind) raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot that will be 600, I am getting 3:1 odds. Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Small Blind opening range. I have position for the rest of the hand and a significant chip lead over Flashlight, making a call the most standard and effective play.

I have top pair, top kicker (Aces with King kicker) and a flush draw with the King of Spades. This is an exceptionally strong hand on this texture. I should lead out with a bet to extract value from weaker Aces, middle pairs, or draw-heavy hands that may call. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard size that allows me to build the pot and control the action.

On this Ace-high board (As 8d 3s), the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) is likely to have a range advantage. I have Qh 9d, which is just Queen high with no real draws or potential to improve significantly. Flashlight's 300 bet into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. Since I have no piece of this board and my hand has very little equity against a typical small blind opening range on an Ace-high texture, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

6d-Td is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. While being on the button grants me positional advantage, the risk-to-reward ratio of opening this hand is unfavorable, as it performs poorly against calling ranges or 3-bets from the blinds. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

In a blind-on-blind situation after the button folds, A5o is a solid hand to open-raise. I want to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down now or lead on the flop. A 3x raise to 300 is standard.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open. This is a premium hand, and I want to 3-bet for value and to take the initiative. By raising to 1000, I put pressure on the Small Blind's opening range, punishing looser opens and building a pot where I will have position post-flop.

I opened the Small Blind to 300 with A5o and was 3-bet by the Big Blind (Pro) to 1000. While A5o is a decent opening hand, it plays poorly against a 3-bet from a player in position who likely has a stronger range (pairs, high broadways, or better Aces). Calling out of position makes it difficult to realize equity on most flops. Since I have 11,000 chips, I don't want to commit 1,000 chips to a marginal spot out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term play here.

King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand to open with from the button in a three-handed game. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to apply pressure to the blinds and maintain an aggressive opening strategy while in position.

Holding 6-2 offsuit in the small blind facing a raise from the button, my hand is extremely weak and completely unplayable. The optimal and standard move here is to simply fold and save my chips for a better spot.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind against a button open is a strong holding that warrants a 3-bet. By raising to 900, I can take the betting initiative, maximize value if ahead, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop against a wide button opening range. This size also puts pressure on the opponent while remaining disciplined.

In a three-handed game, King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand. After my initial raise to 300 was 3-bet to 900 by Flashlight in the Big Blind, I am getting roughly 2:1 odds to call (600 more to win a total pot of 1850). While KJo can be dominated by an opponent's 3-betting range, in a short-handed environment, these ranges are typically wider. I also have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. I will call to see the flop rather than folding a top-tier hand or risking a 4-bet against a stack that is roughly 55 big blinds deep.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a significant range advantage. Although the flop of 9d 5d Jh does not connect well with my Ace-Queen, it is a board texture where a continuation bet is standard to maintain the initiative and represent strength. Betting approximately 40% of the pot (750 chips) allows me to define the hand and potentially take down the pot immediately, while keeping the stack-to-pot ratio manageable if I face resistance.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (KJ) on a board of J-9-5 with two diamonds. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading for about 40% of the pot. Given the three-handed nature of the game, top pair is a very strong hand here. While Flashlight could have an overpair (AA, KK, QQ) or AJ, they could also be betting flush draws, straight draws (like T8s or KQ), or continuation betting with hands like AK/AQ. Calling is the best option to keep their bluffs in and control the size of the pot while I have position, rather than raising and potentially isolating myself against only the hands that beat me.

The turn card, the Ten of Clubs, completes an open-ended straight draw with my Queen-Jack holding (board: 9-10-J). Coupled with an overcard (Ace), this gives me significant equity to improve to the best hand on the river. By leading out with a bet of 1400, I continue to apply pressure as the pre-flop aggressor and set up a manageable stack-to-pot ratio for a potential shove on the river, while maintaining fold equity if my opponent is on a marginal hand.

Flashlight continues to lead on a turn card that introduces more straight possibilities (KQ, Q8). However, I have top pair with a strong King kicker. Flashlight's bet of 1400 into a pot of 4750 offers me excellent pot odds (approximately 4.4:1). In a three-handed game, top pair remains a premium hand in this spot, especially when getting such high odds to continue. Flashlight only has 3300 remaining behind, and by calling, I'm essentially committing to seeing a showdown unless the river is extremely unfavorable. Raising would likely force out bluffs and isolate me against hands that have me beaten, so calling is the most profitable line to keep their range wide while I have position.

With Ace-Queen high on a board of 9-5-J-10-J, I have missed my straight draw and am behind any pair or better that the opponent has been calling with. With only 250 chips remaining in a 6150 pot, any bet I make is negligible and unlikely to induce a fold from a range that has shown strength by calling three streets. Checking allows me to see the showdown without committing my remaining chips, which is the most prudent play.

I have improved to Three of a Kind (Jacks) with a King kicker on the river. Flashlight, who 3-bet pre-flop and led both the flop and turn, has checked the river. While my hand is very strong, the board is highly coordinated with straight possibilities (KQ) and potential full houses (99, TT, 55). Since I hold the Jack of diamonds and the board shows the Jack of hearts and Jack of spades, there is only one Jack left in the deck, making it statistically unlikely Flashlight also has a Jack (AJ or QJ). By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and can win against Flashlight's overpairs (AA, KK, QQ) or missed draws, whereas betting would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten, such as a straight or a full house.

With 6s7d offsuit from the button, my hand is too weak to open-raise or call. Flashlight has a short stack (3250) and might jam over a steal attempt, and Flash has a large stack (14000) that covers me. Simply folding is the optimal and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with Ace-four offsuit, which is a playable hand heads-up against the big blind. Given my stack size is smaller than my opponent's, I want to keep the pot controlled and see a flop. Completing the bet is the standard and most prudent play here rather than raising, which might invite an aggressive re-raise that I would have to fold to.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3d. The Button (Pro) folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just completed. Since I have a very weak hand and the option to see a flop for free, checking is the most sensible action. Raising with such a weak hand into a short stack like Flashlight is unnecessarily risky.

The board texture is very dry with two sevens and a deuce. My Ace-high is essentially a bluff-catcher or a weak hand at this stage, and betting into the big blind here does not generate enough fold equity against a range that could easily contain a small pair or even a seven. Given the pot is small and I am acting first out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's reaction.

I am in a limped pot with 8-high on a paired board of 7c-2s-7s. Flashlight checked to me. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve, there is no reason to bet here. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and avoids the risk of getting check-raised by Flashlight's short stack.

The board texture is [7c, 2s, 7s, Tc]. With Ace-high, I have minimal showdown value, but I have no incentive to bet as a bluff. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (tens, sevens, or pairs) and fold out hands I currently beat (air). Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown or induce a check-back from my opponent.

I have 8-high with no draws on a board of 7-2-7-10. Flashlight (SB) limped pre-flop and has checked both the flop and the turn. While I could attempt a bluff, Flashlight's relatively short stack makes them more likely to call with any marginal hand or pair. Since the pot is small and I have no equity, I will check back and take a free river card.

I have top pair with an Ace on the river, but my kicker is weak (4). My opponent has checked through every street, suggesting they likely have a marginal hand or air. Given the small pot size (100) and my weak kicker, checking is the most prudent decision to reach a cheap showdown. A bet would only be called by hands that beat me or tied with me, or it might fold out worse hands that I am currently beating. Checking minimizes risk and secures the likely win.

I have 8-high with no showdown value on a board of 7-2-7-10-A. The pot is very small (200) and Flashlight, who is on a relatively short stack, has checked every street. While the Ace on the river is a good card to represent, the risk of being called by any pair or a slow-played 7 outweighs the small potential gain from winning this pot. Checking back is the most prudent play to minimize losses.

Ts-4s is a weak starting hand with poor playability, especially considering my 34 big blind stack and the fact that I am facing two opponents with much larger stacks. There is no strategic advantage to playing this hand, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with K3 suited after the Button has folded. This is a standard opening spot in a blind-versus-blind situation. K3s has decent equity and playability. Pro (Big Blind) has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands. Raising allows me to take the pot immediately or play a pot with the initiative if called.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, pocket sixes are too strong to fold and play well in position post-flop. A call is standard to set-mine or play against a wide SB range. I prefer flatting to keep the pot manageable and avoid a 4-bet that would force me to fold my equity.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with Kd3d. The flop comes 5c8h8s, which is a very dry, paired board. This board texture generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range more than the big blind's calling range. I have King-high and two overcards to the board's non-paired card. A continuation bet here is standard to take down the pot immediately, as it's difficult for the opponent to have connected with this board unless they have an 8 or a pocket pair. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is an efficient sizing for this purpose.

On a paired board of 5c 8h 8s, my pocket sixes are ahead of a significant portion of the Small Blind's c-betting range, which includes many overcards, backdoor draws, and complete air. Folding is too weak given the 3:1 pot odds and my positional advantage. Raising is counterproductive as it isolates me against stronger hands like 8x or overpairs (99+) while forcing him to fold his bluffs. A flat call is the standard line to keep his bluffing range intact and evaluate on the turn.

I continuation bet the flop on a dry, paired board and was called by the Big Blind, which suggests they have some connection to the board, such as a pocket pair or a five. The turn 9 of clubs is a relatively neutral card but does complete some straight draws like 6-7. Since my King-high has some showdown value against missed draws but is likely behind a made hand, and I don't want to inflate the pot further with a hand that has limited equity, I will check. This allows me to see how the opponent acts and potentially get to a cheap showdown or fold if they bet heavily.

The turn card is a 9c, introducing a second overcard to my pocket sixes and bringing a flush draw. Flash's check suggests he might be giving up with overcards, pot-controlling a marginal hand, or setting a trap. Betting here achieves very little, as it mostly folds out worse hands (like Ace-highs or complete air) while getting called or check-raised by better hands (9x, 8x, overpairs) and strong draws. Checking behind exercises pot control, keeps the pot small with a marginal showdown-value hand, and allows me to see a free river card.

The river 7c is a highly coordinated card that completes potential straights for any hand containing a 6 and also brings a backdoor flush into play. Given that the turn went check-check, Pro likely has a marginal hand with some showdown value, such as a small pair or a 5, which is unlikely to fold to a single river bet on this texture. My King-high has very little showdown value, but checking is the best option to reach showdown cheaply or concede the pot if Pro bets.

The river 7c completes my 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). Flash checking both the turn and the river strongly suggests he doesn't have a flush, a full house, or a ten for a higher straight. Instead, his range looks like a marginal made hand such as an overpair, 9x, or occasionally trips (like A8) that decided to pot control. I have clear value here. I will size my bet around a third of the pot to extract value from these bluff-catchers, as a larger bet might scare them off. If he check-raises, it is an easy fold given the board's straight, flush, and full house possibilities.

I started with Kd3d in the Small Blind and faced the Big Blind (Pro). After a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet on a paired board (5c-8h-8s), Pro called, suggesting they have a piece of the board or a pocket pair. The turn (9c) and river (7c) made the board significantly more dangerous, completing several straights (any hand with a 6) and potential backdoor flushes. After I checked both the turn and river, Pro has bet 400 into a 1200 pot. While the pot odds are 4-to-1, King-high almost never wins here given that Pro called a flop bet, which usually indicates at least a pair or a straight draw. Pro is likely value betting a marginal hand like a 5, a 7, or a small pocket pair. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Q7o. In a three-handed game, the button range is typically wide, but Q7o is a marginal hand. Flashlight in the big blind is relatively short-stacked with 33 big blinds, making them more likely to shove or defend aggressively against an open. Since Q7o doesn't play particularly well against a shove or in a bloated pot post-flop, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

94o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Playing it out of position from the small blind against the big blind is unprofitable. The best play is to simply fold and surrender the 50-chip small blind rather than investing more chips with a bottom-tier holding.
I am on the button with 8d 2s, which is an extremely weak, unplayable offsuit hand. The standard and optimal play is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with no playability. Given my short stack relative to my opponents, I should avoid playing weak hands out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my remaining chips for better opportunities.

With Ace-Three suited on the button, I have a strong enough hand to open the pot and attempt to steal the blinds. Given my short stack relative to the larger stacks of my opponents, I need to play aggressively to increase my equity. An open-raise is a standard move here to put pressure on the blinds.

I have Ace-Queen suited in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight (Button) opened to 250. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (around 31 Big Blinds), a 3-bet is the best course of action to build the pot and potentially isolate the opener. Calling allows the Big Blind to enter cheaply or squeeze. I will raise to 800 to put pressure on Flashlight and take control of the hand.

I hold Ten-Five suited in the Big Blind. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, this hand is far too weak to consider defending. The correct and only sensible action is to fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Three suited, which is a playable hand against a 3-bet. Since I am in position, calling allows me to see the flop and use my positional advantage to navigate the hand. Folding would be too passive given the pot odds and my ability to outplay my opponent post-flop, while a 4-bet shove might be an overreaction to a single 3-bet. Calling 550 to see a flop in an 1150-chip pot is mathematically sound.

The flop [8h 6c 5s] is quite low and connected, which generally favors the caller's range (suited connectors and middle pairs) more than my 3-betting range (premium broadways and high pairs). Flashlight has a short stack with only 1,800 chips remaining and a pot of 1,700, making the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) approximately 1. Given that I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw, I will check to see how Flashlight responds. This allows me to see a free turn if he checks back or decide whether to commit my stack if he shoves, keeping his potential bluffs in the hand.

Flash checked to me after 3-betting pre-flop on a fairly connected board. With Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw, I have minimal showdown value and betting is unlikely to force a fold from a typical 3-betting range. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card for free, preserving my stack given the effective stack-to-pot ratio.

The turn 6d pairs the board, which is a relatively neutral card. Flashlight's check on the 8-6-5 flop suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like a middle pair or an 8) or has missed completely with broadway cards. By checking again, I am controlling the size of the pot with my Ace-high, which still has significant showdown value against his range of missed broadways. It also allows Flashlight to potentially bluff with hands I currently beat or provides me a free look at the river to potentially improve to a pair of Aces or Queens.

Checking behind on the turn is the most prudent course of action. I have Ace-high, which has minimal showdown value, and betting is unlikely to generate value or force a fold from a 3-betting range that is currently ahead. By checking, I control the pot size and realize my equity for free, preserving my short stack for potentially better spots.

The board has run out 8h-6c-5s-6d-2s. Pre-flop, I 3-bet with Ace-Queen hearts and Flashlight called. Since then, both of us have checked the flop and the turn. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in this spot, as Flashlight's checking sequence suggests they likely hold missed broadway cards or perhaps a weak marginal pair they are afraid to bet. By checking the river, I allow myself to get to a cheap showdown. If Flashlight bets, I will have to decide if they are bluffing with a hand like King-high, but betting myself doesn't accomplish much since I only get called by hands that beat me (like an 8 or a pocket pair).

The river has been checked to me and I hold Ace-high. My hand has very low showdown value on this board, as any pocket pair or better from my opponent's 3-betting range will beat me. Betting is unlikely to generate value or fold out a better hand, as my opponent could easily have a pair of eights, sixes, or even mid-pocket pairs that would call. Checking behind is the standard play to reach showdown and minimize further losses.

I am on the button with 4s 5c. This is a very weak hand. Flashlight in the Big Blind has a short stack of 25 BB (2,500 chips), which makes them more likely to shove over an opening raise. Since 45o doesn't have much playability or equity against a jamming range, and I want to preserve my chip lead, folding is the most prudent play here.

I have 2c 5s offsuit in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack of 25 big blinds and could easily shove or defend aggressively against a steal attempt. There is no justification to commit more chips with this trash hand, so the optimal play is to simply fold.

Holding pocket Tens on the button is a very strong starting hand. I will open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to build the pot and thin the field. If either blind 3-bets, I have a strong enough hand to continue or even 4-bet, especially considering the SB's shorter stack.

I am in the small blind with 10-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise on the button from Pro, I am out of position and have no incentive to defend such a poor holding, especially given my short stack of 26 big blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. With a stack of 2600 (26 big blinds) and holding 5-6 offsuit on the button, this hand is too weak to commit chips. It lacks high-card strength and potential to connect well enough to justify calling the 100 chip investment, especially given the tournament context where preserving stack size is vital for finding better opportunities.

Flashlight folded from the button, leaving me in the Small Blind against Pro in the Big Blind. Jack-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or play post-flop with the betting lead.
I am in the Big Blind with 94o, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, this is an easy fold.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I want to maintain pressure in a three-handed game, this hand has almost no playability. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and could easily shove over a raise. There is no reason to get involved with junk here.

With K2o in the Small Blind against a 25bb stack in the Big Blind, the hand is too weak to play profitably out of position. Raising risks facing a 3-bet shove, and limping leads to playing a weak, easily dominated hand out of position. Folding is the most straightforward and solid play.

Ten-Five suited is too weak to open, even from the button. With the small blind sitting on a 26 BB stack and capable of reshoving, and the big blind covering me, folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

With Ace-Queen offsuit, I have a premium hand heads-up against the big blind. My stack is quite short (2600) compared to the others, so I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips. I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four of diamonds. Flashlight, who is the short stack with 2400 chips, has raised to 250 from the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see the flop. On a flop of Kh 9d 3d, I have a flush draw with my Ten-Four of diamonds. Flashlight, the short stack, has made a continuation bet of 250 into a 500 pot.
