Given that I have position and the odds are favorable, a call is the most appropriate play to see if I can improve on the turn without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a short stack who might be pot-committed with any King.

The board has become more coordinated with the Jack of clubs on the turn, and while I picked up a gutshot straight draw, I still only have Ace-high. On the turn, I still have a flush draw with my 10d 4d. Flashlight has checked, which could signal a weak King, a Nine, or a total air hand. If I hit the flush, I can value bet; if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff based on the river card and Flashlight's action.

The board has missed my hand completely, leaving me with only Ace-high. My opponent has demonstrated a tendency to call bets throughout the session, making a bluff on the river highly unprofitable. Checking is the most sensible play to minimize losses and either reach a cheap showdown or fold if the opponent decides to bet.

I missed my flush draw and am left with Ten-high, which has virtually no showdown value. Flashlight, the short stack, has checked the turn and the river after betting the flop. Jack-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a short stack and no action before me, there is no strategic reason to commit chips. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my remaining stack for a stronger hand.

It's folded to me in the Small Blind, and I have Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard raising hand in a blind vs blind scenario. I will raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) to exert pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to take down the pot pre-flop or go to the flop with the betting lead.
K5s is a reasonable suited hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Small Blind open. We are getting good pot odds and will have position post-flop, making a call the standard play.

We flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a somewhat draw-heavy board (two spades). Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the small blind, our hand is certainly strong enough to call and see a turn in position. Raising would overplay our hand, isolating ourselves against better kings or strong draws, while folding is far too tight.

I have improved to top pair with the Ace of Hearts on the turn. While my kicker is weak (4), the Ace is a very strong card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. On the flop (Ks-Ts-2d), the Big Blind called my continuation bet, suggesting they likely have a King, a Ten, or a draw (like QJ or spades). The Ace might be a scare card for a King, but I still want to extract value from draws and middle pairs that might peel one more street. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to charge the draws and protect my hand, representing a strong Ace or better.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a terrible card for my top pair with a weak kicker. Facing a sizable double-barrel of 800 into 1200, my hand is now essentially just a weak bluff-catcher, drawing nearly dead against any value hand. Folding is the most prudent and clear-cut decision.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand (KQo) in a three-handed game. I have a significant stack lead over Flashlight and a slight lead over Pro. Queen-Ten suited is a very playable hand that performs well post-flop. Facing a standard Button raise, I can 3-bet or call. I flopped the nut straight (8-9-T-J-Q) on a very wet, coordinated board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the best play to allow him to continuation bet. This board hits his range well, and he will likely fire with overpairs, pairs, or various draws. Once he bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and charge any flush or lesser straight draws.

The flop Jh 8s 9h is very coordinated and likely hits the Small Blind's calling range well. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (any Ten). The turn brings a third heart, completing possible flush draws, which makes my straight vulnerable. Since the flop checked through, I need to take the betting lead to extract value from one-heart hands, top pairs, two pairs, or sets. Betting now also protects my hand from a fourth heart or board-pairing river card that could kill my action or beat me. I'll size my bet to around 60% of the pot to charge draws appropriately.

The turn brings a third heart to an already highly coordinated board (J-9-8-5). My opponent checked the flop and is now leading for nearly 60% of the pot on a card that completes flushes and several straight combinations. While I have a gutshot to a straight (needing a Ten) and two overcards, my actual equity is likely very poor. Even hitting a Ten could be dangerous if it's the Ten of hearts. Given the wet board texture and my opponent's show of strength, folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button with KJo, which is a strong starting hand in this position. The standard play is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds, steal the dead money, or play a pot in position. I'll make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind.

Holding a 3-6 offsuit in the small blind, I am significantly out of position and the hand strength is extremely weak. There is no strategic value in calling or re-raising against a button open here, so folding is the only profitable long-term decision.

On a board of Ks 9c Td, I have Ace high with a weak kicker and no direct draws. This board is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range (Button), as it hits many broadway cards and pairs. I flopped top pair with a good kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a very connected rainbow board. Flash checked to me. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws. On a board of Ks 9c Td, I have Ace-high with no direct draws or pairs. This board texture is extremely favorable for the Button's opening range, which contains many King-X, Queen-Jack (straight), and other Broadway combinations. Given that I am out of position and have very little equity to continue against a 300-chip bet into an 850-chip pot, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I am on the button with KJo, a strong hand in this position. Given my short stack relative to my opponents, I need to be aggressive to build it back up. Opening with a raise is the best way to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 2s. Flashlight, the short stack with about 27.5 BBs, has raised to 250 from the Button. I am in the Big Blind with 83 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button to 250, I have no equity to justify calling or three-betting. Folding is the optimal play here to preserve my stack.

I have a strong pocket pair (99) on the button in a 3-handed game. This is well within my opening range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds, especially considering Flashlight is relatively short-stacked at around 30 big blinds.
I am dealt 6-2 offsuit in the small blind, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button, the only logical action is to fold.

J2 suited is a weak starting hand to defend the big blind with against a button raise. Being out of position with a short stack requires playing tighter, and there is no compelling reason to invest more chips into the pot with this holding.
I am on the Button with A5 suited, which is a strong starting hand. Raising is standard to steal the blinds, and A5s plays very well post-flop if called. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the small blind with a pocket pair of sevens (7d, 7s). With a stack of 3000 and the blinds at 50/100, I have 30 big blinds. Given the stack depth and the current pot odds, calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop and potentially hit a set, rather than overcommitting with a 3-bet shove this early, although a 3-bet could be considered if the opponent's opening frequency is high. I will call the 200 to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fives. The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. With 4:1 pot odds and a pocket pair, this is a clear call to set-mine. Flashlight is short-stacked, which increases the implied odds if I hit my set against him, though it also limits the total payout from that specific player.

I am out of position in a three-way pot on an Ace-high board. My pocket sevens are currently losing to any Ace, any eight, or any higher pocket pair, which are all well within the pre-flop raising range of the button (Pro). Since I have no pair and no draw, betting would be an ineffective bluff against two opponents. Checking is the standard play to see if the action remains passive or to fold if someone bets strongly, as I cannot improve to a winning hand unless I spike a seven, and even then, I might still be behind a higher pair or set.

I have pocket fives on an Ace-high flop after calling a pre-flop raise. The board (Ah 8h 2d) is quite dangerous for my small pair, as it is very likely one of my opponents has an Ace or an Eight. Since the Small Blind (Flashlight) checked, I will also check to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) acts. My plan is to fold to any significant bet, as my hand has very little potential for improvement and is currently just an underpair.

I have top pair with a weak kicker, plus a backdoor diamond draw. The board is wet with two hearts, and both opponents have checked to me. A continuation bet here serves both for value against worse aces or middle pairs, and for protection against flush and straight draws. I'll size my bet around 40% of the pot to charge the draws while controlling the size of the pot in case I face resistance.

The board is Ace-high with an eight, and my pocket sevens have no pair and no realistic path to improve to a winning hand against two opponents. With two opponents still in the hand and a bet of 300 to call, continuing without a pair on this texture is strategically incorrect. I have pocket fives on an Ace-high board (Ah 8h 2d). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has bet 300 into a pot of 750 after the Small Blind checked and then folded to the bet. Pro's range heavily includes Aces, and my pocket fives are now just an underpair to both the Ace and the Eight. I have very little equity to improve (only two fives remaining in the deck) and calling here would likely lead to more difficult decisions on later streets if Pro continues to bet. Given the board texture and the action, folding is the most prudent play.

With 28 big blinds, my stack is relatively short compared to the opponents, making it important to play aggressively in position. Kh7h is a playable suited hand from the button, suitable for a standard opening raise to attempt to steal the blinds and increase my stack. A raise to 250 is standard for this blind level and gives me a good chance to take down the pot pre-flop or define the strength of my opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4h. While these are suited connectors, they are at the very bottom of the range. Our hand, 8s 5h, is far too weak to defend even against a potentially wide button raise. The pot odds are not good enough to justify calling with an unsuited, unconnected hand that plays poorly post-flop. I will simply fold.

I am on the button with pocket threes (3c 3d). In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand, especially from late position. J3o is a very weak starting hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button and being out of position in the Big Blind, the only correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 72o, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having the chip lead, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

84s is too weak to play from the small blind, even if folded to me. The big blind has around 40 big blinds, a stack size where they can easily defend or 3-bet jam over steals. Folding is the safest and most standard option.

J7s is a marginal suited hand. While opening a wide range from the button in a 3-handed game is standard, J7s can easily be dominated and often leads to tricky post-flop decisions. Given the stack sizes, with the Big Blind covering us and the Small Blind sitting on around 40 BBs, a fold is the safest and most optimal choice to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h-3c, which is a very weak starting hand. Since the Button has folded, I am facing the Big Blind. While I have already posted 50 chips, there is no reason to invest further chips into the pot with such a poor hand, as it will be difficult to play post-flop. The best decision is to fold and forfeit the 50 chips to preserve the rest of my stack.

I am in the button position with King-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially since I am short-stacked relative to my opponents. Given the strength of the hand and my position, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 5h, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Flashlight) has already folded. Although it is only 50 more for me to call the Big Blind, this hand has very poor playability and I am out of position against Pro, who has been relatively active. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to conserve chips.

I am on the button with Qs 4s, a marginal suited hand. While opening from the button is standard in a 3-handed game, Flashlight (BB) has shown a lot of pre-flop aggression and has a relatively short stack of 4,050 (40.5 BB). This makes me susceptible to a 3-bet shove or a wide defense. Given my current chip lead and the tournament's midpoint, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity to avoid unnecessary variance against an aggressive short stack.

With King-Six offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a reasonable hand to attempt a steal. Raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure on the Big Blind, who is sitting on a ~40 BB stack. If he three-bets, I can comfortably fold. Playing aggressively from the SB helps pick up uncontested pots over the long run.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The small blind has raised to 250. I have a decent Ace to defend against a steal attempt from the small blind. The pot odds are favorable to call, so I will take a flop to see if I can hit an Ace or better.

I completely missed this paired board with King-Six offsuit, and I am out of position. The board of 9-J-J hits many of the Big Blind's calling range combinations, including straight draws, broadway floats, or pairs. A continuation bet is unlikely to fold out better hands and getting called or raised puts me in a tough spot with no equity. Checking to evaluate his action is the safest route; if he bets, I can easily fold.

The flop is 9d-Jh-Js, a paired board where I have Ace-high. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked, which could signal weakness, a slow-played Jack, or a hand they are looking to pot-control. Betting here does not accomplish much as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or get value from worse hands, and I risk being check-raised. Checking behind is the standard play to see the turn for free and re-evaluate.

The turn card adds a Ten to the board, making it 9-J-J-T. This puts four to a straight out there and adds a diamond flush draw. My hand is essentially King-high with a gutshot to a Queen. This board connects heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, and attempting a delayed c-bet bluff here is very risky, as it's unlikely to fold out any pair or draw he might have picked up. Checking controls the pot size and allows me to fold cleanly if he bets, or safely see a river card if he checks back again.

The board is 9d-Jh-Js-Td, and I hold Ah-4c. I am currently behind any pair, and with a paired board, the likelihood that my opponent has a Jack or has improved to a straight is non-negligible. Betting here is unlikely to force a fold from a better hand and only inflates a pot I am currently losing. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and reach the river, where I can re-evaluate based on the final card.

On the river, the board is 9-J-J-T-8. My King-high is weak and only beats missed lower draws or pure air that didn't connect with the board. Since my opponent checked both the flop and the turn, they could be trapping with a weak pair, holding a missed draw, or have Ace-high, which beats me. Bluffing into this board is dangerous, as any 7, Q, or pair will easily call. Checking allows me to see a free showdown where I might occasionally win against worse missed draws, and safely fold if my opponent bets.

The board is 9d-Jh-Js-Td-8c, and I hold Ah-4c. My Ace-high is very weak, and the board is highly coordinated, meaning my opponent could easily have a pair, a straight, or even a better Ace. Since my opponent checked, checking behind is the best move to reach showdown without further risk. I cannot expect to be called by any hand worse than mine, so value betting is not an option.

8-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very low equity against any reasonable calling range. Being first to act on the button pre-flop, folding is the standard and most profitable choice to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-6 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Given my stack size (4350) and the fact that I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against a player with a much larger stack, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play. There is no reason to invest further chips with such a low-equity hand.

I am on the button with 8d, 6c, which is a weak hand with very little playability. With a significantly shorter stack than my opponents, I need to be selective and wait for better opportunities to enter a pot, rather than wasting chips with a speculative hand. Folding is the disciplined play.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Queen-Eight offsuit (Q8o) is a relatively strong hand in a 3-handed game, specifically when it's folded to the blinds. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to attempt to steal the blinds or take control of the hand against Pro, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Ten-Seven offsuit is too weak to call or 3-bet bluff with against a standard open. Folding is the most profitable and standard decision here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a strong suited hand (Qh9h) in a 3-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table, and I want to exert pressure, especially on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size in this game and allows me to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

JTo is a marginal hand and we are in the Small Blind, meaning we will be out of position for the rest of the hand post-flop. Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, calling is unprofitable due to reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act and could squeeze. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation from the big blind against a button raise. I have enough equity to 3-bet here to take control of the hand and put pressure on the button, who likely has a wide opening range. Raising to 900 effectively bloats the pot with a premium hand and discourages the opponent from seeing a cheap flop with weaker cards.

I am on the button with Qh9h and faced with a 3-bet from the big blind (Flashlight) to 900. I have already committed 300. To call the remaining 600, I am getting roughly 2:1 odds (600 to win a total pot that will be 1850). Given that I have position on the aggressor and my hand has good playability post-flop, calling is the most profitable play. Flashlight's stack is relatively short (around 35bbs total), so I should be wary of any further aggression on the flop, but folding here would be too tight given the price and position.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on a dry board of 6h-3s-Tc. Since I initiated the 3-bet pre-flop, I maintain the initiative. A continuation bet here is standard to represent a strong hand and take down the pot if the opponent missed, which is likely given the raggy cards. I'll bet 750 into the 1850 pot to apply pressure.

After calling a 3-bet pre-flop with Qh9h, I have missed this dry flop completely. Flashlight, who has a relatively short stack, is leading into the pot with a continuation bet of 750 into 1850. Given their short-stack status, they are likely pot-committed and will shove most turns. I have no pair and no direct draw, only two overcards to the low cards on the board and a backdoor flush draw. Against a standard 3-betting range that includes JJ+, AT+, and various Broadway hands, my equity is insufficient to justify a call here, especially with the risk of facing a shove on the next street. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

T7s is a marginal hand to open from the button 3-handed. While it can be a steal candidate, Flash is in the Big Blind with a covering stack and has been playing relatively aggressively. Folding this two-gapper is a disciplined choice to avoid being put in tough post-flop spots or facing a 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with King-Eight offsuit after the button (Pro) folded. The big blind (Flash) is the only opponent remaining. My hand is marginal but playable in a heads-up situation. Since I have already posted 50, I will call the remaining 50 to see the flop and potentially take the pot if I hit or if the opponent misses.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Small Blind, Flashlight, has just completed the blind rather than raising. With an Ace in my hand and being in position for the rest of the hand against a player with a relatively short stack (52BB), I have a strong enough hand to check and see a flop for free. Raising is an option to exert pressure, but checking allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a range that might be trapping or just weak. I will check to see the flop.

I am in the small blind with King-high and no pair or draw on a Queen-high board. Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the next street. Betting would likely force me to fold to a raise or bluff into an opponent who potentially has a Queen.

Flashlight checked the flop after limping pre-flop. My Ace-high has some showdown value in a limped pot, but the board of Q-9-2 doesn't hit my hand. There's no need to inflate the pot with a weak hand. I'll check back to see the turn for free.

The turn card, the seven of hearts, does not improve my King-high hand. Since the opponent checked back the flop, they likely have a marginal holding or a draw that they are also playing cautiously. Betting into them now would likely only be called by better hands or induce a raise, forcing me to fold. Therefore, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

We are in a limped pot that has been checked through on the flop. The turn card is the Seven of Hearts, and Flashlight has checked for a second time. My hand, Ace-high, still possesses reasonable showdown value in a three-handed limped pot where no one has shown any aggression. Betting here might force out hands I already beat, but it could also lead to a check-raise from a trapping hand or a call from a marginal pair that won't fold for one bet. By checking back, I keep the pot small, minimize risk, and get to see the river for free to either improve or reach showdown.

The board has been checked down completely on all streets. My King-high hand has no showdown value against any pair, but checking is the best option because I cannot realistically get a better hand to fold by betting, and any worse hand that would call a bet is already likely checking behind. Taking the showdown with a check is the most prudent path.

In a three-handed limped pot that has been checked through on every street (flop, turn, and river), my Ace-high (Ah 6d) holds significant showdown value. Flashlight's repeated checks suggest a very weak range, likely missed high cards or a very marginal pair. While the river brought a third spade, Flashlight's check indicates they probably don't have a flush or a strong hand. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and while it might fold out some weak pairs, the pot is too small (200 chips) to justify the risk of a check-raise or a call from a hand that beats me. Checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and win the pot against other air hands.

8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low equity. With only 52 blinds in my stack compared to my opponents' larger stacks, I need to prioritize playing tighter and more selective ranges. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 7h. The Button has folded. Against the Big Blind (Pro), who has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #82), a raise is a standard play to attempt to take down the blinds. Q7o is a decent enough hand to semi-bluff with here, as it has some high card value if called, but the primary goal is to win the 150 chips currently in the pot without seeing a flop.

I hold Ace-King suited, a premium starting hand, and I am facing a raise from the Small Blind. I want to 3-bet to build the pot in position, extract value, and take the initiative going into the flop. A 3-bet to 900 (3x the initial raise) is a standard and strong sizing.

I initially raised from the Small Blind to 300 with Q7o as a standard blind-stealing attempt. However, Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind. Q7o is a very weak hand that does not play well out of position against a 3-betting range. Calling would be unprofitable in the long run, and 4-betting as a bluff is unnecessarily risky at this stage. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game, J3o is too weak to open for a raise, especially with a relatively active Big Blind (Flashlight) and a competent Small Blind (Pro). I will fold and wait for a better spot.
With 76 offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to profitably play out of position against the big blind. Folding is the standard and most optimal decision here to preserve my stack.

We are first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game. Ace-three offsuit is a reasonable hand to open-raise with, as it contains an Ace blocker and we will have a positional advantage post-flop if called. A standard raise to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds to potentially take down the pot right now, while risking a minimal amount.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit, which is a marginal holding OOP against a button open. Given my short stack relative to my opponents and the positional disadvantage, calling is not ideal as it leads to difficult post-flop situations. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 4c. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. The pot is currently 400, and it costs me 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, meaning I need about 27% equity to justify a call. Suited hands like J4s generally have enough equity against a wide button opening range (often around 38-40%) to make a defend profitable, despite being out of position. Given the deep stacks and the price, I will call and see a flop.

On this flop of Kd Td 7d, I have Jack-high with a backdoor club flush draw. This board significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Button) range, as it contains several high cards and a flush draw. As the player in the Big Blind and out of position, the standard play with a hand that missed the flop is to check and see how the opponent reacts. Betting into the pre-flop raiser on this texture with just Jack-high would be a low-frequency, high-risk bluff that is unlikely to be profitable.

We completely missed the flop, having only Ace-high with no direct or strong backdoor draws. The board of 7d Td Kc is fairly wet and connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's pre-flop defending range, including flush draws, straight draws, and various one-pair hands like Kings or Tens. A continuation bet here would be a pure bluff with very low equity when called. Checking behind is the most prudent play to see a free turn card or potentially realize our Ace-high showdown value.

The turn 6h is a blank card that doesn't improve my hand. I have Jack-high with no draw. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back on a King-high flop, which could indicate a marginal made hand like a Ten or a Seven, or perhaps they are playing a King cautiously. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to continue if faced with a bet, checking is the most prudent play. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, hoping to see a free river card.

The turn card is a 6h, which doesn't change the board texture significantly. Flash has checked again, indicating weakness and likely a marginal made hand or a draw. While a delayed continuation bet could fold out some weak pairs like 7x or pocket eights, our Ace-high still retains some showdown value against missed draws. By checking behind, we control the size of the pot, keep our range relatively protected, and get a free look at the river where we can potentially win at showdown or reassess.

I have Jack-high with no showdown value on a board that has been checked through by the pre-flop aggressor. While the double check from Pro suggests weakness, my hand is too weak to win at showdown. However, bluffing into this board after checking twice myself is unlikely to be successful as it is difficult to represent a strong hand that would have played this way. I will check and hope for a check-back, or fold to any bet, as the pot is small and preserving my stack is more important than a low-equity bluff.

On the river, Flash checks for a third time. The board of 7d Td Kc 6h 8c missed all diamond draws and some straight draws. Our Ace-high has decent showdown value against these busted draws. Given our passive line of checking the flop and turn, a river bet looks suspicious and might easily be called by a stubborn small pair like an 8, 7, or 6. Since we have showdown value and bluffing is risky, checking behind to see if our Ace-high is good is the best action.

Queen-four offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, even when holding the button. There is no incentive to invest chips with this marginal holding in a three-handed game. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

I am in the Small Blind with King-high (Kh 5c). Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. King-high is relatively strong in a heads-up blind-vs-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and attempt to win the blinds immediately, or play the hand with the lead if called.

Holding King-Ten suited in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open. KTs is a very strong hand to defend and plays excellently in position. Calling is a solid choice to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the flop, though a 3-bet is also a viable option. Given the pot odds and position, calling is standard and keeps his wider opening range in.

I raised pre-flop and the flop comes with three hearts. I hold the King of hearts, which gives me a draw to the nut flush (as the Ace of hearts is already on the board). I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to represent strength—either an Ace or a completed flush—and to maintain the initiative. If I am called, I still have significant equity to improve to the best possible flush on the turn or river.

I defended the Big Blind with Kd Td against a Small Blind raise. The flop comes a monotone Ah 8h 2h, and I have no hearts, no pair, and no realistic draw. Facing a continuation bet on this board texture, I have virtually zero equity and no reasonable path to win the hand. This is an extremely straightforward fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. While it is a standard late-position opening opportunity, this specific hand is quite weak (J5o) and does not play well post-flop if called. Given the stack sizes and the recent history of the blinds defending or playing aggressively, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

We are in the Small Blind with 87o, a marginal unsuited connector. Flash folded on the button. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with a weak offsuit connector is generally unprofitable, so the standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand. While we are on the button, there's no need to force a steal here when we have no reads that the blinds are over-folding. We can wait for better hands to commit chips and avoid playing a weak offsuit connector post-flop against aggressive opponents.

I am in the small blind with 4cTs, a weak hand. The pot is currently 150 (my 50 and the big blind's 100), and I need to call 50 to see the flop. Since the button (Pro) folded, I am facing only the big blind (Flash). Given the favorable pot odds and the fact that I am in position post-flop if the big blind checks, calling is a reasonable play to see a flop cheaply.

I have been dealt Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. The action folded to Flashlight in the Small Blind, who merely completed the bet. This usually indicates a weak range or a desire to see a cheap flop. With a hand this strong, I must raise for value and to take initiative. I will raise to 400 to build the pot and capitalize on my significant card strength advantage.

I am in the small blind holding 4cTs, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Flash has raised to 400 after I put in my small blind. Calling 350 more chips out of position against an aggressive raise with this holding is mathematically suboptimal and will likely lead to difficult post-flop situations. I am on the button with A2 offsuit. The small blind (Flash) has raised to 400. This is a weak, unsuited hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position if I call. Given the stack depths and the opponent's aggressive tendencies, playing A2o is generally a fold in this spot as it is dominated by many of the opponent's raising hands. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with T4o. The Button (Flashlight) has already folded. I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop against Pro in the Big Blind. While T4o is a weak hand, getting 3:1 odds to see a flop in a head-up situation is generally acceptable, though checking is also an option if I were the BB. Since I am the SB, I can either call the 50 or fold. Given the stack depths and the low cost, I will call to see if I hit the flop.

With K9s in the Big Blind against a Small Blind limp, I have a strong suited hand and positional advantage post-flop. Raising to 300 puts pressure on his capped limping range, often winning the pot pre-flop or taking the betting lead into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. After I completed the blind, the Big Blind (Pro) raised to 300. I would need to commit 200 more chips to see the flop. T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a raise. While I have already put in 100 chips, those are a sunk cost. Calling here would likely lead to more difficult decisions on the flop with a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

54o is a very weak hand that lacks both high card value and significant connectivity. While being on the Button provides a positional advantage, this hand is below the standard opening range in a 3-handed game, especially with aggressive opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 72s, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the button folding, making a play from the small blind with such a disconnected and low-ranking hand is unnecessary, as it plays terribly post-flop if called. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

Holding King-Six offsuit (K6o) on the button is generally a weak starting hand. While it can sometimes be used as a steal attempt, the risk of getting called or 3-bet by the blinds with dominating hands (like better Kings or any Ace) is too high. Folding is the most standard and solid play here.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. With A-7 offsuit, I have a decent hand to attack the big blind's range. As the short stack at the table, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips rather than playing passively. Raising to 250 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or continue with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q5o. Flashlight, the small blind, has raised to 250. While the pot odds are roughly 2.33 to 1 to call, Q5o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Given Flashlight's frequent raising and the fact that I want to maintain my healthy stack, I will choose the conservative and standard play of folding here.

I am holding Ten-Seven offsuit on the button. This is a very weak hand that lacks both high card strength and suited/connected potential. Given my stack size of 5300 and the need to play more conservatively to recover from my short-stack position against opponents with significantly larger stacks, calling or raising with this holding is not mathematically sound. Folding is the standard, low-variance play.

I am holding 6d 2s in the small blind. The button has folded, leaving only me and the big blind. 6-2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand. Although it only costs 50 chips to complete the bet, playing this hand out of position against Pro is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7-6 offsuit. In a 3-handed game, while position is valuable, 7-6 offsuit is near the bottom of an opening range and doesn't play well post-flop against two opponents who have been active. Pro (SB) recently 3-bet me when I opened light, and Flashlight (BB) is short-stacked and may look for a spot to shove. Given my healthy stack and the marginal nature of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my position in the tournament.

With T4s in the small blind and the button folding, this hand is too weak to play, even if I only have to complete or try a steal. Out of position against the big blind with such a marginal holding, folding is the most fundamentally sound and profitable choice.

K8o is a marginal offsuit King, and while an open raise from the Button in a 3-handed game is an option, it is near the bottom of my opening range. I prefer to wait for slightly better hands to commit chips, avoiding potentially tricky post-flop scenarios if called or 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with A5s, and the Button (Pro) has folded. This is a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. Given that I have a significantly shorter stack than both opponents, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips. A5s is a strong hand in a heads-up pot, and raising is the best way to leverage my position to either take the pot immediately or set up a post-flop continuation bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 93o. The Small Blind has raised to 300. While this is a blind vs blind situation, 93o is too weak to defend even against a wide Small Blind range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8c 5s, which is a weak starting hand. The pot is 150 (my 50 small blind/big blind implied cost is usually 100/200, but here it looks like a limp situation). I need to call 100 to see a flop. Given my stack is 5450, I am the short stack and should avoid playing weak junk. However, the cost is relatively low and I am on the button. Still, playing this hand is negative EV in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s6d. Flashlight has folded from the Button. The pot is currently 150 (100 from the Big Blind, 50 from my Small Blind). It costs me only 50 more to call, and the minimum raise is to 200. While 76o is a speculative hand, the pot odds for a call are 3:1 (50 to win 150). However, playing out of position against 'Pro' (who has a similar stack size to mine) is difficult. Given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 97/150) and my healthy stack of 12,150, I can afford to fold weak offsuit connectors to avoid tricky post-flop spots against the Big Blind, or I could complete to see a flop cheaply. Considering 'Pro' has been active, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to avoid being squeezed or outplayed out of position.

I am on the button with J9o, a reasonable hand to enter the pot with against two opponents. However, the current pot is small (150) and the cost to enter is only 50 more chips (since I have already posted the 50 chip small blind equivalent in terms of position relative to standard button play, but here I am the button and it's 50/100, so I need to put in 100 to call or more to raise). In a three-handed game, J9o is strong enough for an open-raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked (5350) compared to Pro and me. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately.

J7s is a marginal hand and playing it out of position from the small blind against a 3x button raise is generally unprofitable. The hand lacks the equity and playability needed to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff, so folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind with a suited King. The button has raised to 300, and I have 100 already in the pot. The pot odds are very favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), making this an easy defend. I will call to see a flop, as K3s plays reasonably well post-flop, and I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding.

The flop is QsQhQd, which is an extremely static, wet board where the absolute nuts is essentially guaranteed by anyone holding a Queen (quads). Since I hold Kd3d, I have effectively nothing. Leading into this board is nonsensical as it only isolates me against a range that beats me, and I have no equity to speak of. Checking is the only standard play.

On a board of Qs-Qh-Qd, the likelihood of anyone having a Queen is low, but possible. I have J9o, which currently is Jack-high but effectively playing with three Queens on the board. Flashlight checked to me. There are very few hands Flashlight would check-call with here that don't beat me (like a Pocket Pair or a King-high), but a bet might induce a fold from similar air or small underpairs if I represent the Queen or a high pocket pair. However, checking back allows me to see a turn for free and potentially realize equity if a Jack or Nine falls, or to see if Flashlight leads out on the next street. Given the dry board, I will check to keep the pot small.

The board is QsQhQdTs. The board is extremely static with three Queens already on the board. Any Queen has made quads, and any King or Ace is just a high card. Since I hold Kd3d, I have effectively no showdown value and no equity to improve against any part of an opponent's calling range. Betting accomplishes nothing as it will only be called by better hands or hands that are already crushing mine, while folding out hands that are already worse (though unlikely to get worse than King high). Checking is the standard play to navigate to the river as cheaply as possible or induce a bluff if the opponent is feeling weak.

The board shows trip Queens and a Ten. My current best hand is three Queens with a Jack and Ten as kickers. The board is QsQhQdTs8d. I hold Kd3d. The board features four Queens, meaning the primary hand ranking is determined by the kicker. I have a King high with a 3 kicker. Betting here serves no purpose as it will only be called by superior hands, and I cannot force a better hand to fold. Checking is the only logical play to see if the opponent is willing to show down their own weak holding, keeping the pot manageable and minimizing risk.

On a board of Q-Q-Q-T-8, my hand is effectively trip Queens with a Jack kicker (Q-Q-Q-J-T). Flashlight has checked all three streets after calling a pre-flop raise. I am on the Button with 9-2 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Pre-flop, the standard and mathematically correct play with this hand is to fold.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Eight of Clubs in the Small Blind. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind with pocket tens on the button, my stack depth of roughly 48 big blinds makes 4-betting risky and potentially over-committing to a range that likely dominates or flips against me. Calling is the superior play as it maintains pot control, allows me to leverage my positional advantage post-flop, and keeps me in the hand to see if I can navigate a favorable board.

I am holding 8cTc in the Small Blind. After my initial call of the Button open, the Big Blind (Pro) has squeezed to 1100 and the Button has called. Checking preserves my stack, controls the pot size, and allows me to either realize my equity for free or evaluate the action safely if someone bets.

The flop is extremely dry (5-5-2), and my pocket tens are currently a strong overpair. With both opponents checking, this is a prime opportunity to bet for value and to protect my hand against overcards that could improve on the turn. A bet of 1100, roughly one-third of the pot, allows me to extract value from holdings like overcards (AK, AQ) that might still be in the hand, while also defining my position against the pre-flop aggressor. Given the stack depth, this sizing allows me to maintain some flexibility on the turn while building the pot now.

I am holding 8c Tc on a very dry paired flop of 5h 5d 2s. I have completely missed this board and have no draws. With Ace-Jack high on a dry, paired board, I have completely missed the flop and only have two overcards along with a backdoor diamond draw. Flashlight's bet into two opponents after calling a pre-flop squeeze indicates strength, likely a mid-pocket pair, A5s, or at least better overcards like AK/AQ. Given his relatively short stack, a check-raise bluff would have virtually zero fold equity as he is likely pot-committed with his value hands. Floating out of position with just Ace-high is unprofitable. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 5-2 offsuit on the button, this is a trivial fold. While I have positional advantage, 5-2 is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and does not warrant a raise or a call, especially against active opponents who are capable of 3-betting or defending their blinds effectively.
With Q7o in the small blind and the button folding, this hand is too weak to play profitably out of position against the big blind. Folding is the standard and safest play.
I am on the button with 3d 5s, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Raising or calling here would be a long-term losing play. The standard and optimal choice is to fold.

I am in the small blind and the button (Pro) has folded. Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 3x. King-Ten is a strong starting hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While a 3-bet is a viable option to take control, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that often dominates the Small Blind's wider opening range while maintaining pot control against a fairly aggressive opponent.

I am in the small blind and raised pre-flop, which was called by the big blind. The flop comes 3-4-9 rainbow, which completely misses my Ace-Jack high. On this dry, low-card flop (3s 4h 9d), my King-Ten offsuit still has significant showdown value against the Small Blind's wide opening range. The board is 3-4-9-6 rainbow with a heart draw. I hold Ace-Jack high. I checked the flop and the opponent checked behind, suggesting neither of us has a strong connected hand. On the turn 6h, Flashlight has checked for a second time. My King-Ten offsuit still holds some showdown value against his range of missed high cards and air. I have Ace-high on a board that contains a King. The pot is 900 and the opponent has bet 300 on the river, offering 4:1 pot odds. My Ace-high is a pure bluff catcher. I have bottom pair (fives) on a King-high board in a three-way pot. The bet from the button (Pro) is small, but the board is disconnected and I am out of position. On the river with Ace-high (Ac4c) on a board of 2c-Kh-5s-9s-Tc, the action was checked through on the turn after a flop continuation bet from the button. Holding 3d-6s, I have a very weak hand with low equity. As I am in the button position, opening this hand would be loose and unprofitable against two opponents with larger stacks. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I hold Jack-Five offsuit in the big blind, which is a very weak hand against an open raise from the small blind. Given that my cards have poor playability and are easily dominated, the pot odds do not justify a call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with Jack of Spades and Nine of Spades. With the button having folded, I have a clear opportunity to see a flop cheaply by completing the big blind. The flop is 8c-2d-5c, and my Js-9s has no pair or significant draw. Betting as a bluff here would be ineffective as it's unlikely to fold out a better hand, and I'd rather check to see a turn card cheaply.

I have Ten-high on an 8-2-5 board with two clubs. I have no pair and no significant draw. Flashlight checked to me. Holding Ace-Four offsuit on the button, this is a strong enough hand to open the pot in a three-handed game. I am currently the shortest stack at the table and need to be proactive to chip up. I have been dealt pocket Queens, which is a premium starting hand. Flashlight opened from the button, a position where players often raise with a wide range of hands. I am in the Big Blind with K3o. Flashlight opened from the Button and Flash 3-bet from the Small Blind. K3o is a very weak hand, and facing significant pre-flop aggression, it's an easy fold.
